---
title: "What it looks like to use AI on your actual files"
subtitle: "AI Bootcamp — Session 1"
author: Emily Beam
date: 2026-04-22
format:
  revealjs: default
---

# Terminal

## What you're seeing

1. **A terminal app** — where you type commands (this one is Ghostty, but any terminal works)
2. **Claude Code** — an AI assistant that runs in the terminal
3. I type a request, the AI responds, I iterate

The key difference from chat: it can **read and write files** on my computer. I don't copy-paste anything in — it reads the file directly.

## Markdown: the output format

AI tools write in Markdown — a simple plain-text format that uses `#` for headers and `-` for bullets.

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Raw file**

![](assets/markdown-raw.png){width="100%"}
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Rendered in Typora**

![](assets/markdown-pretty.png){width="100%"}
:::
::::

You do not need to learn the syntax. The practical point: the AI may hand you a `.md` file, and a viewer like **Typora** ($15) makes it look like normal formatted text.

## Context and iteration

**Context window**: The AI only "sees" what is in the current session. Be explicit about what you want.

**Iteration:**

- The first output is a draft, not a final product
- Refine: "Make it shorter," "Add an example," "This is wrong because..."
- Save to a file so you can pick up later

<!-- EMILY: Add a specific example here. Something like: "I asked it to extract due dates from my syllabus. First pass missed the final exam. I said 'you missed the final exam date on page 3.' It fixed it." -->

::: {.alert-box}
**When it's wrong:** It will confidently cite a paper that does not exist. Check everything — especially numbers, citations, and code. I am not exaggerating.
<!-- EMILY: Replace or supplement with your best real hallucination/failure story. -->
:::

# Demo: Create your writing voice

## You don't have to write the instructions yourself

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
A "voice file" is a plain Markdown file that describes how you write — tone, structure, habits. The AI reads it and matches your style.

**You don't write it from scratch.** You give the AI samples of your real writing — emails, letters of recommendation, feedback — and ask it to describe your patterns.

The result is a file like `EB-VOICE.md` that you can edit, refine, and reuse across projects.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**The prompt:**

```
Read these five emails I've sent.
Describe my writing style: tone,
sentence length, how I open and
close messages, what I avoid.
Save it as EB-VOICE.md.
```

**What you get back:**

```markdown
# EB Voice
- Tone: direct, warm, slightly
  informal
- Opens with context, not greeting
- Short paragraphs, rarely > 3
  sentences
- Avoids: hedging, exclamation
  points, corporate jargon
```
:::
::::

## Then use it whenever you want

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
Once the voice file exists, you can:

- Tell the AI to **load it automatically** via standing instructions
- **Call it on demand**: "Draft this email using my voice file"
- **Update it** as your preferences change — it's just a text file

**Tip:** Keep a central folder for all your prompts, voice files, and project instructions. They accumulate fast and you don't want them scattered across your computer.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Using it:**

```
Draft a reply to this student
asking for a deadline extension.
Use my voice file. Be kind but
firm — the deadline stands.
```

**Output looks like you wrote it** — not like a chatbot wrote it. That's the point.
:::
::::

::: {.alert-box}
**Outlook limitation:** AI agents cannot currently interact with the Outlook desktop app — Microsoft gates this heavily. However, tools like Claude Code have direct integrations with Gmail and Google Calendar that allow reading, drafting, and even scheduling. The email demo here uses saved files, not a live inbox connection.
:::

# Next Steps

## Before next time (April 27)

- Try using an AI tool for **one real task** this week
- Think of a research or teaching task you'd like to automate
- Install guide: [eabeam.github.io/uvmecon-ai/prep.html](https://eabeam.github.io/uvmecon-ai/prep.html)

**Next session:** Research pipelines, slide creation, web scraping, automated grading

## Resources

- Bootcamp site: [eabeam.github.io/uvmecon-ai](https://eabeam.github.io/uvmecon-ai)
- Claude Code docs: [docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code)
- UVM Copilot (free): [go.uvm.edu/copilot](https://go.uvm.edu/copilot)
